questions.q: What is the difference between metrics, logs, and traces in observability?
options: Metrics are numeric measurements; logs are text records of events; traces show request flows across systems
Metrics are for production while logs are for development
Traces are old; modern systems only use metrics
They are three names for the same monitoring data
questions.explanation: Observability's three pillars: metrics (time-series data like CPU%), logs (event records), and traces (request paths). Environment (B) doesn't divide them; traces remain essential (C); they're distinct data types (D).

questions.q: What is an SLO (Service Level Objective)?
options: A target for system reliability, such as 99.9% uptime, guiding engineering priorities and alerting thresholds
A guarantee that services will never fail
The speed at which alerts are sent to on-call engineers
A list of all services running in production
questions.explanation: SLOs define acceptable performance targets; they're not guarantees (B), alert delivery speeds (C), or service inventories (D). SLOs enable trade-offs: you don't need 99.999% for non-critical systems, freeing resources for other improvements.

questions.q: What is the difference between an SLO and an SLA?
options: An SLO is a target to guide engineering; an SLA is a contract promising consequences for breaches
An SLA is internal; an SLO is for customers
They are identical terms used interchangeably
An SLA applies to one service; an SLO applies to the entire system
questions.explanation: SLOs are internal targets used for operational decisions; SLAs are binding contracts (with penalties). Usage (B) is sometimes reversed; they're distinct concepts (C). Scope (D) isn't the difference—SLAs can be per-service too.

questions.q: What is alerting in the context of monitoring?
options: Triggering notifications to on-call engineers when metrics breach predefined thresholds based on SLOs
Recording all application events for later analysis
Preventing problems by rolling back code automatically
Storing historical metric data for trend analysis
questions.explanation: Alerting notifies humans when conditions require intervention; it's triggered by thresholds tied to SLOs. Recording events (B) is logging; automatic rollback (C) is remediation, not alerting; data storage (D) is time-series databases.

questions.q: What are RED metrics in monitoring?
options: Rate (request throughput), Errors (failure rate), and Duration (latency) for measuring service health
Real-time, Embedded, and Distributed monitoring approaches
Redundancy, Efficiency, and Durability for storage systems
Resource, Environment, and Deployment for infrastructure
questions.explanation: RED focuses on user-facing service health: how many requests (Rate), how many failed (Errors), how long did they take (Duration). The other options are not monitoring frameworks—they describe different concepts entirely.

options: A record showing how a single request flows through multiple services, showing latency at each hop
A backup of all database transactions
A log file containing traces of deleted user accounts
A network diagram showing connections between servers
questions.explanation: Distributed tracing tracks requests across microservices with latency at each step, enabling performance diagnosis. Database backups (B), user activity logs (C), and network diagrams (D) are unrelated.

questions.q: What problem does structured logging solve compared to unstructured logs?
options: Structured logs (JSON key-value pairs) are machine-parseable, enabling precise filtering, aggregation, and alerting
Structured logs are more human-readable than plain text
Structured logs use less disk space
Structured logs are only needed for distributed systems
questions.explanation: Structured logs enable automated parsing and searching (free-text logs require regex hacks). Readability is debatable (B); disk impact is negligible (C); benefits apply to any system (D).

questions.q: What is cardinality in monitoring, and why is it a concern?
options: The number of unique label values in a metric; high cardinality (e.g., per-user metrics) causes storage and query performance problems
The accuracy of metric measurements
The number of distinct services being monitored
The frequency at which metrics are recorded
questions.explanation: Cardinality refers to unique combinations of labels; unbounded cardinality (e.g., one metric per user ID) explodes storage and slows queries. Accuracy (B), service count (C), and sampling frequency (D) are separate monitoring concerns.

questions.q: What is the purpose of health checks in monitoring?
options: To verify a service is functioning correctly and responding, enabling orchestrators to restart or remove failed instances
To scan for security vulnerabilities in code
To measure user satisfaction with the application
To verify backups were created successfully
questions.explanation: Health checks signal readiness and liveness, enabling self-healing. Vulnerability scanning (B) is separate; user satisfaction is NPS or feedback (C); backup verification (D) is a different mechanism.

questions.q: What is the difference between a counter and a gauge metric?
options: A counter monotonically increases; a gauge can go up or down, reflecting current state at a point in time
Counters measure failures; gauges measure successes
Counters are for logs; gauges are for metrics
Counters are deprecated in favor of gauges
questions.explanation: Counters track cumulative totals (request count, errors); gauges show current state (memory usage, temperature). Success/failure (B) is separate from metric type; they're both metric types (C, D incorrect).
